perturbation.original_text: Recipient must comply with all applicable laws, regulations and rules in its jurisdiction , including but not limited to those relating to the export of information and data.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient must use at least the same standard of care in protec ting the confidentiality of the Confidential Information as it uses in protecting its own information of a sim ilar nature and, in any event, no less than a reasonabl e standard of ca re. Recipient must comply with all applicable laws, regulations and rules in its jurisdiction , including but not limited to those relating to the export of information and data. Recipient must notify Discloser promptly upon discovery that any Confidential Information has been accessed or otherwise acquired by or disclosed to an unauthorized person.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the compliance clause to a section that discusses the standard of care diminishes its visibility, which is problematic because failing to comply with export laws can result in penalties, and these requirements are now less discoverable. This especially harms McMaster University as a research institution that must follow strict compliance regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Export and Import Permits Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. E-19
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the Export and Import Permits Act outlines penalties for contravening the Act or any regulation made under it, making the explicit and clear compliance with these regulations critically important. By obfuscating the compliance clause under 'Standard of Care', its enforceability is reduced.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Our History Share Facebook X (Twitter) LinkedIn U.S. Census Bureau History U.S. Census Bureau History History of the U.S. Census Bureau Quick Links Related Information Agency History and Timeline Monthly History and the Census Stories Decennial Census Official Publications: 1790-2020 Decennial Census of Population and Housing Questionnaires & Instructions: 1790-2020 Genealogical Research with Census Records The 72-Year Rule Privacy and Confidentiality at the U.S. Census Bureau Our People: Census Directors and Notable Alumni A Timeline of Census History A visual chronology of the history of the census from 1790 to the present. View Timeline Factfinding is one of America’s oldest activities. In the early 1600s, a census was taken in Virginia, and people were counted in nearly all of the British colonies that became the United States at the time of the Revolutionary War. (There also were censuses in other areas of the country before they became parts of the United States.) Following independence, there was an almost immediate need for a census of the entire Nation. Both the number of seats each state was to have in the U.S. House of Representatives, and the states’ respective shares in paying for the war were to be based on population. Census History Story of the Month Monthly History Story July 2025: Science Fiction July 01, 2025 Monthly History Story June 2025: Juneteenth June 01, 2025 Monthly History Story May 2025: Charlie Chaplin May 01, 2025 Monthly History Story April 2025: The Vietnam War April 01, 2025 View All Monthly Stories Explore Census Bureau History Our Stories Oral histories by retiring or retired Census Bureau employees provide a special insight into census operations and planning. The 72-Year Rule The U.S. government will not release personally identifiable information about an individual to any other individual or agency until 72 years after it was collected. Facts by Decade Get a quick portrait of the U.S. by decade with pop culture milestones, population highlights, census details, and the 10 largest urban places. Survey and Census History Learn the history of our different censuses, surveys, and statistical programs, from the Decennial Census and Economic Census to demographic and more. Census Artifacts Learn more about some of these interesting artifacts and their importance to the censuses and surveys we conduct. Teacher and Student Resources Census data can give students a deeper understanding of historic events. Data@Museums Data@Museums shows how demographic, agriculture, and economic census data are used to educate visitors. Census Bureau Data Collection Innovations As the science of statistics advanced, the Census Bureau changed and updated its methodology. Availability of Census Records Individual census records from 1790 to 1950 are maintained by the National Archives and Records Administration, not the U.S. Census Bureau. Publications related to the census data collected from 1790 to 2020 are available in the Census Bureau Library. Visit the National Archives Web site to access 1940 and 1950 Census records. Decennial census records are confidential for 72 years to protect respondents' privacy. Records from the 1950 to 2010 censuses can only be obtained by the person named in the record or their heir after submitting form BC-600 or BC-600sp (Spanish). Online subscription services are available to access the 1790–1950 census records. Many public libraries provide access to these services free of charge to their patrons. Contact your local library to inquire if it has subscribed to one of these services. perturbation.justification: The original contract text contains a clear compliance clause, stating, 'Recipient must comply with all applicable laws, regulations and rules in its jurisdiction , including but not limited to those relating to the export of information and data.' The changed text moves this clause into a section discussing the standard of care, which diminishes its visibility and importance. This creates a structural flaw where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, which harms McMaster University as a research institution. Section 13 of the Export and Import Permits Act outlines penalties for contravening the Act. By obfuscating the compliance clause under 'Standard of Care', its enforceability is reduced.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient must notify Discloser promptly upon discovery that any Confidential Information has been accessed or otherwise acquired by or disclosed to an unauthorized person.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient must notify Discloser promptly upon discovery that any Confidential Information has been accessed or otherwise acquired by or disclosed to an unauthorized person. This Agreement represents the enti re agreement between the parties with regard to the Con fidential Inform ation and supersedes any previous understandings, commitments or agreements, whether written or oral.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the required notification of unauthorized access of confidential information to the 'Entire Agreement' section buries this crucial clause. This hinders the 'Discloser' ability to respond quickly and mitigate damages related to data breaches and could create legal liability. There should be a clear clause on the procedure for notification of a breach of confidentiality, and by placing this at the end of the contract, the procedure is weakened and thus is less enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, S.C. 2000, c. 5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10 describes the principle of safeguarding personal information. While this NDA might not directly deal with 'personal information' as defined by PIPEDA, the underlying principle of promptly addressing data breaches to protect sensitive information is analogous. Placing the notification requirement in an obscure section undermines the prompt action needed to safeguard confidentiality, contradicting the spirit of Canadian data protection laws.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires prompt notification of unauthorized access to confidential information. The changed text buries this requirement in the 'Entire Agreement' section, which weakens the enforceability and clarity of the notification procedure. The Law Explanation refers to PIPEDA, which although not directly applicable, reflects a broader legal standard requiring prompt action to safeguard confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'safeguards' as a 'fair information principle' and guidance for businesses to comply with the law. While it doesn't explicitly state where to put this notification, the Perturbation Explanation makes a good argument that placing this notification clause in an obscure section undermines the prompt action needed, as opposed to giving it its own section, thus contradicting the spirit of data protection laws.
